questions.stem: A maintainer is told to rewrite an application's UI from scratch in a new framework, discarding the old codebase entirely. By the lecture's premise, why is this NOT 'software change' in the canonical sense?

options.text: Software change always operates on existing code, whereas this is green-field development
Software change excludes any UI work by definition
Software change can only be corrective, never perfective
Software change forbids switching frameworks under any circumstances

questions.explanation: Software change is adding new functionality to EXISTING code; building anew while discarding the old code is green-field development, the very thing SC is contrasted with. (SoftwareChange p.2)

questions.stem: Which of the following is NOT one of the four Lientz & Swanson maintenance categories?
questions.explanation: The Lientz & Swanson categories are perfective, adaptive, corrective, preventive. 'Incremental' is an impact-on-functionality type, not a Lientz & Swanson category. (SoftwareChange p.3-4)

questions.stem: A bank must update its software because a government changes the sales-tax calculation rules nationwide. Using Lientz & Swanson, this change is best classified as which category?
questions.explanation: Adapting software to a changed external environment (like the Y2K example) is adaptive maintenance; the law change is an environmental shift, not a bug fix or new feature. (SoftwareChange p.3)

questions.stem: Two teams each spend a sprint: Team X adds a new 'export to PDF' feature; Team Y untangles a tangled class hierarchy so future features are easier, with zero user-visible change. By Lientz & Swanson, how do these map?

options.text: Team X is adaptive; Team Y is corrective
Team X is preventive; Team Y is perfective
Team X is perfective; Team Y is preventive

questions.explanation: Adding/extending user functionality is perfective; improving code structure to forestall future problems is preventive. (SoftwareChange p.3)

questions.stem: If perfective maintenance dominates (~50%), what does that imply the change process should be optimized for?

options.text: Adding functionality to existing code, not merely patching defects
Removing obsolete code as the primary activity

questions.explanation: Because perfective (adding/improving capability) dominates, the process is tuned for adding functionality to existing code rather than just fixing bugs. (SoftwareChange p.3)

questions.stem: A change removes a deprecated 'fax export' option that no customer uses anymore. Classified by impact on functionality, which type is this?
questions.explanation: Removing obsolete functionality is a contraction; incremental adds, replacement swaps, refactoring changes structure without behavior change. (SoftwareChange p.4)

questions.stem: Which statement correctly distinguishes the 'why' axis from the 'how' axis of classifying a change?

options.text: Both axes classify by why, just at different granularities
Lientz & Swanson classify by how; impact-on-functionality classifies by why
Lientz & Swanson classify by why (perfective/adaptive/corrective/preventive); impact-on-functionality classifies by how (incremental/contraction/replacement/refactoring)
Both axes classify by how the user perceives the change

questions.explanation: Lientz & Swanson taxonomy answers WHY a change is done; impact-on-functionality answers HOW it affects functionality. The guide explicitly contrasts these two axes. (SoftwareChange p.3-4)

questions.stem: The guide notes a conceptual link between one 'why' category and one 'how' type because they usually go together. Which pairing is it?

options.text: Corrective maintenance is typically realized as Replacement
Adaptive maintenance is typically realized as Contraction
Perfective maintenance is typically realized as Refactoring
Preventive maintenance is typically realized as Refactoring

questions.explanation: Preventive maintenance (improving structure to prevent future problems) is typically carried out as refactoring (changing structure without behavior). (SoftwareChange p.4)

questions.stem: In the eight-phase model, which phase immediately FOLLOWS Concept Location?
questions.explanation: Concept Location feeds Impact Analysis; the classes it finds become the initial impact set consumed by Impact Analysis. (SoftwareChange p.5, p.9)

questions.stem: Which phase immediately PRECEDES Concept Location in the canonical process?
questions.explanation: The order runs Initiation -> Concept Location -> Impact Analysis ...; Initiation precedes Concept Location. (SoftwareChange p.5)

questions.stem: A student lists the implementation-group phases as 'Postfactoring, Actualization, Prefactoring'. What is wrong?

options.text: Postfactoring is not an implementation phase
Actualization belongs to the design group, not implementation
The order is reversed; it should be Prefactoring, Actualization, Postfactoring
Nothing is wrong; that is the correct order

questions.explanation: The implementation group runs Prefactoring -> Actualization -> Postfactoring (refactor before, do the change, refactor after). The student reversed it. (SoftwareChange p.5)

questions.stem: Which phase is grouped as an 'interaction with the world' alongside Initiation?
questions.explanation: Interactions with the world = Initiation and Conclusion; the others are design or implementation phases. (SoftwareChange p.5)

questions.stem: A teammate insists Verification is a single discrete phase that runs once, right after Postfactoring. Which correction is accurate?

options.text: Verification runs vertically alongside Prefactoring through Conclusion, not as one discrete step
Verification replaces the Conclusion phase entirely
Verification is optional and usually skipped

questions.explanation: Verification spans the implementation phases vertically (Prefactoring -> Conclusion), guaranteeing correctness throughout; it is not a single final step. (SoftwareChange p.5, p.13)

questions.stem: If a maintainer skipped Prefactoring entirely and went straight to Actualization, what risk does the guide imply they take on?

options.text: The new code cannot be committed to version control
The change's impact is not localized/minimized beforehand, so it may ripple more widely
The concept can no longer be located in the code

questions.explanation: Prefactoring is opportunistic refactoring done BEFORE the change to localize/minimize its impact; skipping it leaves the change less contained. (SoftwareChange p.10)

questions.stem: During Actualization, a maintainer adds new code and then must update neighboring classes so the system stays consistent. What is this act called?
questions.explanation: Change propagation is the act of updating neighbors to keep the system consistent; the ripple effect is the cascading of changes through dependencies. (SoftwareChange p.11)

questions.stem: Which best contrasts 'change propagation' with the 'ripple effect' in Actualization?

options.text: They are identical terms used interchangeably
Change propagation is automatic; the ripple effect is manual
Change propagation happens in Conclusion; the ripple effect happens in Initiation
Change propagation is the deliberate act of updating neighbors; the ripple effect is the cascading phenomenon of changes spreading through dependencies

questions.explanation: Actualization names both: change propagation (updating neighbors to stay consistent) and the ripple effect (changes cascading outward through dependencies). (SoftwareChange p.11)

questions.stem: After integrating a new feature, a method has grown to do far too much. Which phase removes this, and what is the anti-pattern called?
questions.explanation: Postfactoring eliminates anti-patterns introduced by the change, e.g. long method and bloated class. (SoftwareChange p.12)

questions.stem: If prefactoring cleans code up before a change goes in, which later phase plays the symmetric clean-up role afterward?
questions.explanation: Prefactoring cleans up before the change to localize impact; postfactoring cleans up after to remove introduced anti-patterns. They mirror each other. (SoftwareChange p.10, p.12)

questions.stem: Which activity belongs to the Conclusion phase rather than any other?

options.text: Extracting domain concepts from the request
Locating the concept in the code
Committing to version control and building the new baseline
Updating neighboring classes after writing new code

questions.explanation: Conclusion commits the finished code, builds the new baseline, and prepares for the next change. (SoftwareChange p.14)

questions.stem: Put these in correct phase order: (i) commit and build new baseline, (ii) extract concepts from the request, (iii) grow the impact set, (iv) refactor to remove a bloated class introduced by the change.
questions.explanation: Concept Location (extract concepts) -> Impact Analysis (grow impact set) -> Postfactoring (remove bloated class) -> Conclusion (commit/baseline). (SoftwareChange p.5-14)

questions.stem: Which single statement about the phase grouping AND Verification is fully correct?

options.text: Design = Concept Location + Impact Analysis; implementation = Prefactoring + Actualization + Postfactoring; Verification spans the implementation phases
Design = Initiation + Conclusion; Verification spans only Initiation
Implementation = Concept Location + Impact Analysis; Verification spans the design phases
Design = Prefactoring + Postfactoring; Verification is a single step after Conclusion

questions.explanation: Interactions with the world = Initiation/Conclusion; design = Concept Location/Impact Analysis; implementation = Prefactoring/Actualization/Postfactoring; Verification runs vertically across the implementation phases. (SoftwareChange p.5, p.13)

questions.stem: A change request arrives, requirements are captured, and the requests are prioritized. Which phase is this?
questions.explanation: Initiation is where a change request arrives, requirements are captured, and requests are prioritized. (SoftwareChange p.6; ChangeInitiation p.2)

questions.stem: Which trigger for a change typically maps to CORRECTIVE maintenance?

options.text: A user asks for an enhancement
A manager wants to match a competitor's feature
A user reports a software bug
A programmer proposes a structural improvement

questions.explanation: A user reporting a bug triggers corrective maintenance; enhancements/competitor parity are perfective; structural improvements are preventive. (ChangeInitiation p.3)

questions.stem: Which of these is NOT a valid form a change request may take, per the Change Initiation deck?

options.text: A UML class diagram of the whole system

questions.explanation: The deck lists three forms: a sentence/paragraph, a bug report, or a user story. A full UML class diagram is not one of them. (ChangeInitiation p.4)

questions.stem: A product owner writes one user story covering login, password reset, two-factor setup, and account deletion on a single card. Per the deck's rule, what is the correct response?

options.text: Split it into several user stories because it cannot fit a 3x5 card
Keep it as one story; size limits are only advisory
Convert it to a bug report instead
Move it directly to Concept Location without splitting

questions.explanation: A user story must fit a 3x5 card; if new functionality cannot fit, it has to be divided into several user stories. (ChangeInitiation p.4)

questions.stem: A teammate must add an item to the backlog. Which wording is the only one that follows the deck's user-story template?

options.text: The system shall persist wishlist items in the database within 200ms
As a registered shopper, I want to save items to a wishlist so that I can buy them later
Wishlist feature: add table, add endpoint, add UI button
Bug: wishlist button throws a null pointer on click

questions.explanation: The canonical template is 'As a [user type], I want [goal] so that [reason]'; the others are a spec, a task list, and a bug report. (ChangeInitiation p.5)

questions.stem: In the Product Backlog's MoSCoW-style tiers, which is a valid priority tier named by the deck?
questions.explanation: The deck's tiers are Must have / Nice to have / Won't have. (ChangeInitiation p.7)

questions.stem: A backlog item is reworded and another is deleted two sprints after it was added. Which deck rationale explains why the backlog keeps shifting like this?

options.text: Version control rewrites it on every commit
The compiler regenerates it during each build
It is locked after Initiation and never changes
Users acquire additional knowledge and developers need additional clarification as understanding evolves

questions.explanation: The backlog evolves because additional knowledge is acquired by users and additional clarification is needed by developers over time. (ChangeInitiation p.7)

questions.stem: In the end-to-end Change Initiation flow, what does the new code combine with to reach the desired code?
questions.explanation: The flow plugs New Code into Existing Code to reach Desired Code. (ChangeInitiation p.8)

questions.stem: The deck illustrates prefactoring with two Fowler refactorings that both pull behaviour outward into a new type. Which pair are they?

options.text: Pull Up Field and Push Down Method
Replace Conditional with Polymorphism and Move Method

questions.explanation: The deck names Extract Class (gather members into a new component class) and Extract Superclass (create a new abstract class) as prefactoring examples. (SoftwareChange p.10)

questions.stem: A maintainer wants to gather scattered fields and methods into a brand-new component class before making a change. Which prefactoring refactoring is this, and why do it before?

options.text: Extract Superclass, to remove a long method after the change
Extract Class, to verify correctness via walkthroughs
Extract Class, to localize and minimize the change's impact
Inline Class, to grow the impact set

questions.explanation: Extract Class gathers members into a new component class; prefactoring is done before the change to localize/minimize impact. (SoftwareChange p.10)

questions.stem: Verification comprises testing and walkthroughs. Which three kinds of testing does the deck list?
questions.explanation: The deck lists functional, unit, and structural testing (plus walkthroughs) under Verification. (SoftwareChange p.13)

questions.stem: Because Conclusion commits the finished code and builds a new baseline, it is the natural hook into which two later course themes?
questions.explanation: Conclusion (commit, build new baseline) ties into Version Control / Git and CI. (SoftwareChange p.14)

questions.stem: A consultant claims the eight-phase model treats Concept Location and Impact Analysis as 'implementation' work because both touch code. Why is the claim wrong?

options.text: Neither phase exists in the model
Concept Location and Impact Analysis are the DESIGN group, not implementation; implementation is Prefactoring/Actualization/Postfactoring
Both are world-interaction phases, not design
Impact Analysis is design but Concept Location is world-interaction

questions.explanation: The grouping puts Concept Location + Impact Analysis in SC design; only Prefactoring, Actualization, Postfactoring are SC implementation. (SoftwareChange p.5)

questions.stem: You receive the request 'Correct error that arises when trying to paste a text.' What is the FIRST thing concept location asks you to do?

options.text: Write all the unit tests for paste and text
Commit a new baseline to version control
Find the code snippet where the change must be made

questions.explanation: Concept location finds the code snippet where a change is to be made, starting from domain concepts in the request. (ConceptLocation p.2)

questions.stem: Why does the vocabulary gap make concept location necessary at all?

options.text: Code is always written in the same natural language as the request
Requests never mention any domain concepts
The compiler removes all concept names before runtime
Change requests are usually phrased in domain-concept terms, but those concepts are scattered and renamed inside the code

questions.explanation: Requests are formulated in domain concepts, but those concepts are scattered across and renamed inside the code, so they must be located. (ConceptLocation p.2)

questions.stem: The deck's 'visiting a large city' analogy is used to justify which idea about comprehension?

options.text: You should learn only the route to your destination (partial, as-needed comprehension), not every street
You must read every file before making any change
Large programs can be fully comprehended given enough time
Comprehension is unnecessary if you have tests

questions.explanation: The large-city analogy: you learn the route to your destination, not every street — partial, as-needed comprehension. (ConceptLocation p.3)

questions.stem: A new hire asks whether they should read the entire JHotDraw codebase before their first change. What does the deck imply they should expect about comprehending a large program?

options.text: They can always be fully comprehended before any change
They cannot be completely comprehended, so you aim for minimum essential understanding
They require comprehending every class equally
Comprehension is irrelevant to concept location

questions.explanation: Large programs cannot be completely comprehended; the programmer aims for the minimum essential understanding via an as-needed strategy. (ConceptLocation p.3)

questions.stem: In the concept triangle, the edge from Intension to Extension is labeled with which relationship?
questions.explanation: Name->Intension is naming/definition; Name->Extension is annotation/traceability; Intension->Extension is recognition/location. (ConceptLocation p.4)

questions.stem: Given the concept triangle, which edge corresponds to 'annotation / traceability'?
questions.explanation: Name->Extension = annotation/traceability (names trace to instances). (ConceptLocation p.4)

questions.stem: In the Dog/Pes/Hund example, which item belongs to the Extension of the concept?
questions.explanation: Extension = the set of all instances (Fido, Lassie, Buck, a cartoon dog...); 'Hairy animal with teeth' is the intension; 'Hund' is a name. (ConceptLocation p.5)

questions.stem: Why does the Dog/Pes/Hund example matter for concept location?

options.text: Dogs are the most common domain concept in software
The same concept can have different names, so the name you search for may differ from the name used in code
It proves natural-language names are always identical to code identifiers
It shows extensions are always empty in practice

questions.explanation: 'Dog/Pes/Hund' are different names for one concept; the lesson is that a programmer's search name may differ from the code's name, so reason from intension to extension. (ConceptLocation p.5)

questions.stem: Reading the JHotDraw watermark request, the deck pulls out four domain concepts to drive concept location. Which set is it?
questions.explanation: The deck underlines Export, Drawing, Text, and Format as the domain concepts in the watermark request. (ConceptLocation p.6)

questions.stem: Starting from the term 'watermark' and reasoning toward the concrete export classes that realise it counts as which direction around the concept triangle?
questions.explanation: Moving from the definition (intension) to recognizing/locating the actual code instances (extension) is the recognition/location edge. (ConceptLocation p.4)

questions.stem: Which concept-location methodology is being used when a senior developer who already knows the codebase simply points to the right class?
questions.explanation: Human knowledge: a programmer who already knows the code points to the location. (ConceptLocation p.7)

questions.stem: Which is NOT one of the four families of concept-location methodologies?
questions.explanation: The four families are human knowledge, traceability tools, dynamic search, and static search. Compiler optimization is not one of them. (ConceptLocation p.7)

questions.stem: You want to locate the export concept by running JHotDraw, triggering an export, and watching which classes execute. Which methodology is this, and which lab uses it?

options.text: Static search (dependency search), used by the user-story lab
Human knowledge, used by the portfolio lab
Dynamic search (execution traces), used by the IDE-debugger lab
Traceability tools, used by the IDE-debugger lab

questions.explanation: Running the program and observing executed code is dynamic search; the CLLab uses the IDE debugger for exactly this. (ConceptLocation p.7; CLLab)

questions.stem: Which three techniques fall under STATIC search?

options.text: Unit testing, integration testing, and acceptance testing
Dependency search, pattern matching (GREP), and information retrieval

questions.explanation: Static search (no execution) comprises dependency search, pattern matching/GREP, and information retrieval. (ConceptLocation p.7-8)

questions.stem: GREP is an acronym; spelled out in full, the four words are which of these?
questions.explanation: GREP = global regular expression print; it prints lines matching a regular expression. (ConceptLocation p.8)

questions.stem: How is GREP meant to be used as a concept-location technique?

options.text: Once: run a single query and accept whatever lines return
Iteratively: formulate a query, investigate results, then refine the query
By compiling the code and inspecting bytecode
By running the program and tracing execution

questions.explanation: GREP is used iteratively — formulate, investigate, refine. (ConceptLocation p.8)

questions.stem: Why does the lecture pair dependency search WITH GREP rather than relying on GREP alone?

options.text: GREP requires running the program, which is too slow
GREP is blind to dependencies and renaming, so dependency search complements it
GREP only works on compiled binaries

questions.explanation: GREP is fast and tool-free but blind to dependencies and renaming, hence the complementary dependency search. (ConceptLocation p.8)

questions.stem: Which best contrasts a module's LOCAL functionality with its COMPOSITE functionality?

options.text: Local = the module plus its suppliers; composite = only the module itself
Local = public methods only; composite = private methods only
Local = compiled code; composite = source code
Local = concepts implemented in the module itself; composite = the module plus all its supplier modules combined

questions.explanation: Local functionality is what the module implements itself (not delegated); composite is the complete functionality including all supplier modules. (ConceptLocation p.9)

questions.stem: During dependency search you inspect a class: the concept is NOT in its local functionality, but IS within its composite responsibility. What is the correct next action?

options.text: Descend into its supplier modules to continue the search
Stop the search; the concept is located here
Mark it Unchanged and backtrack immediately
Mark it Blank and ignore it

questions.explanation: Concept in composite but not local means descend into suppliers; you only stop when the concept is in the LOCAL functionality. (ConceptLocation p.9-10)

questions.stem: The dependency-search algorithm halts exactly when which situation about the current module holds true?

options.text: The concept is anywhere in the module's composite responsibility
The concept is implemented in the module's local (own) functionality
All supplier modules have been marked Next
The root module has been inspected once

questions.explanation: You stop only when the concept is implemented in the module's local functionality. (ConceptLocation p.10)

questions.stem: A maintainer stops the search the instant a class's COMPOSITE responsibility contains the concept. Which rule did they break, and what is the consequence?

options.text: They violated the backtrack rule; they will inspect too few classes
They violated the marking rule; nothing bad happens
They violated the stop condition (must be LOCAL); they may halt at a delegating class instead of the real implementation
They violated the starting-module rule; the search never begins

questions.explanation: Composite containing the concept means descend into suppliers, not stop; stopping there risks halting at a mere delegator rather than the class that locally implements the concept. (ConceptLocation p.9-10)

questions.stem: Per the CLLab, a student beginning a dependency search for a feature should usually pick their starting class from which layer?
questions.explanation: Features often start from controller classes, so begin the search there. (CLLab p.1)

questions.stem: Running the interactive Computer/Programmer version of the algorithm, the tool pauses for a verdict on whether the concept is implemented in a module. Whose verdict is it?
questions.explanation: The Computer finds candidate module sets; the Programmer judges whether the concept is implemented. (ConceptLocation p.21)

questions.stem: Which search mark means a class was inspected and its composite responsibility CONTAINS the concept?
questions.explanation: Propagating = inspected and composite responsibility contains the concept (so search propagates into it). (ConceptLocation p.11)

questions.stem: Which mark means a class was inspected but its composite responsibility does NOT contain the concept (a dead end)?
questions.explanation: Unchanged = inspected and composite responsibility does not contain the concept (dead end for this path). (ConceptLocation p.11)

questions.stem: Both 'Propagating' and 'Unchanged' apply to inspected modules; on what single criterion do the two marks diverge?

options.text: Propagating means scheduled; Unchanged means never inspected
Propagating means local; Unchanged means composite
Propagating applies to the root only; Unchanged to leaves only
Both mean inspected; they differ on whether the composite responsibility contains the concept

questions.explanation: Both Propagating and Unchanged mean the class was inspected; the difference is whether the composite responsibility contains the concept. (ConceptLocation p.11)

questions.stem: How does the 'Next' mark differ from the 'Blank' mark?

options.text: Next = scheduled for inspection; Blank = neither inspected nor scheduled
Next = contains the concept; Blank = does not
They are synonyms used in alternating slides

questions.explanation: Next means scheduled for inspection; Blank means never inspected and not scheduled. (ConceptLocation p.11)

questions.stem: Which is NOT one of the four search marks used in dependency search?
questions.explanation: The four marks are Blank, Propagating, Unchanged, and Next. 'Pending' is not one of them. (ConceptLocation p.11)

questions.stem: In the deck's worked dependency-search trace, the search is rooted at which class?
questions.explanation: The trace is rooted at UMLEditor, with the five *DiagramGraph classes as direct suppliers. (ConceptLocation p.12)

questions.stem: Tracing the UMLEditor, the algorithm follows one DiagramGraph first as the most likely supplier. Which subclass is that?
questions.explanation: ClassDiagramGraph is chosen as the most promising supplier (bold arrow). (ConceptLocation p.14)

questions.stem: In the UMLEditor trace, following toward which class is flagged as the 'wrong way', and how is it then marked?
questions.explanation: Heading toward NoteNode is the wrong way; on backtrack NoteNode is marked Unchanged (grey). (ConceptLocation p.16-17)

questions.stem: In the UMLEditor trace, after the wrong-way detour to NoteNode is abandoned, which class turns green as the first concept location found?
questions.explanation: ClassNode contains the concept and is the first 'concept location found' (green). (ConceptLocation p.18)

questions.stem: Having reached ClassNode, the UMLEditor trace notes a 'possible extension of the search.' Which classes could it widen into, and why?

options.text: SequenceDiagramGraph / StateDiagramGraph, to restart at the root
RectangularNode / AbstractNode, e.g. to widen the impact
MultiLineString only, to narrow the impact

questions.explanation: The search may continue into RectangularNode/AbstractNode (e.g. to widen the impact); AbstractNode is reached as another location. (ConceptLocation p.19-20)

questions.stem: Reproducing the UMLEditor trace, you descend several suppliers and reach a class whose composite responsibility lacks the concept. What is the correct action AND mark?

options.text: Stop and mark the class Propagating
Continue descending and mark it Next
Mark it Blank and leave it scheduled
Backtrack and mark the class Unchanged

questions.explanation: If the composite responsibility lacks the concept, that path is a dead end: mark the class Unchanged and backtrack. (ConceptLocation p.10-11, p.17)

questions.stem: What does the canonical UMLEditor trace deliberately demonstrate that a 'happy path' example would not?

options.text: A wrong turn followed by a backtrack
That concept location never needs marks

questions.explanation: The trace intentionally takes a wrong way (toward NoteNode), marks it Unchanged, and backtracks — showing backtracking is expected, not failure. (ConceptLocation p.16-17)

questions.stem: In the UMLEditor CDG, ClassNode extends RectangularNode. On a class-dependency graph, what kind of edge does that 'is-a' represent?
questions.explanation: ClassNode is a RectangularNode is a generalization/inheritance relationship in the CDG. (ConceptLocation p.12)

questions.stem: Your dependency search just hit a dead-end class and you had to back up a level. Per the deck, how should you regard this backtrack?

options.text: A sign the algorithm was applied incorrectly
A reason to abandon concept location entirely
An expected, normal part of the search — not a failure
Proof the concept does not exist in the code

questions.explanation: Backtracking is expected, not failure; the canonical trace deliberately includes a wrong way and backtrack. (ConceptLocation p.16-17)

questions.stem: Order the dependency-search steps correctly: (i) select a module, (ii) find the set of starting modules, (iii) check whether the concept is in the local module, (iv) if not local but in composite, find supplier modules.
questions.explanation: Find starting modules -> select one -> is the concept local? -> if no but in composite, find suppliers. (ConceptLocation p.10)

questions.stem: Once concept location finishes, the classes it found move forward to the next phase playing what role there?

options.text: Impact Analysis, as the initial impact set

questions.explanation: Concept-location classes become the initial impact set consumed by Impact Analysis. (SoftwareChange p.9)

questions.stem: A student says 'concept location already produces the full impact set.' Why is that wrong?

options.text: Concept location yields nothing; the impact set comes entirely from Initiation
Concept location yields only the INITIAL impact set; Impact Analysis grows it via dependency analysis
The impact set is produced during Conclusion, not either phase
Concept location and impact analysis are the same phase

questions.explanation: Concept location finds the starting classes (initial impact set); Impact Analysis grows that set by analyzing dependencies. (SoftwareChange p.8-9)

questions.stem: You have finished stepping through JHotDraw in the debugger for the CLLab. Which artifact do you now write up for the portfolio?

options.text: A full UML sequence diagram of the system
A list of all GoF patterns in the framework
An initial set of classes in a Domain Class / Responsibility table

questions.explanation: The CLLab deliverable is the initial set of classes written as a Domain Class / Responsibility table. (CLLab p.1)

questions.stem: During the CLLab, a debugger run surfaces a long list of classes. Which subset should the student retain?

options.text: Only the framework's AWT base classes

questions.explanation: If many classes appear, keep only the domain classes for the initial set. (CLLab p.1)

questions.stem: A maintainer GREPs for 'export', 'write', 'format' to seed candidate files, then runs the debugger starting from the export Tool. How is this combination best characterized?

options.text: Combining a static pattern-matching search with a dynamic execution-trace search
Using two dynamic searches in sequence
Using human knowledge plus traceability tools
Replacing dependency search with information retrieval

questions.explanation: GREP is static pattern matching; the IDE debugger is dynamic execution-trace search — the example combines both methodologies. (ConceptLocation p.7-8; CLLab)

questions.stem: Arrange the methodology families along a spectrum running from 'leans on a person's memory' toward 'examines code without executing it'. Which order is correct?

options.text: Static search -> dynamic search -> traceability tools -> human knowledge
Human knowledge -> traceability tools -> dynamic search -> static search
Dynamic search -> static search -> human knowledge -> traceability tools
Traceability tools -> human knowledge -> static search -> dynamic search

questions.explanation: Human knowledge (memory) -> traceability (links) -> dynamic (run it) -> static (no run). (ConceptLocation p.7-8)

questions.stem: Best choice: a feature spans many delegating classes and you have the source but cannot run it. Which static technique most directly follows the call structure?

options.text: GREP for a single literal keyword
Dependency search over the Class Dependency Graph

questions.explanation: Dependency search uses the CDG to follow supplier relationships statically — best when you have source but cannot execute. (ConceptLocation p.9)

questions.stem: Dependency search reasons over a static structure mined from the existing code. Which structure is that?

options.text: Control-flow graph of a single method

questions.explanation: Dependency search is a static method using Class Dependency Graphs extracted from the existing code. (ConceptLocation p.9)

questions.stem: You start at a controller, find the concept is in the composite (not local), descend through two suppliers, hit a dead-end class, backtrack, then find a supplier whose LOCAL functionality implements the concept. What marks do the controller, dead-end, and target classes end with?
questions.explanation: The controller is inspected with the concept in its composite (Propagating); the dead-end's composite lacks it (Unchanged); the target locally implements it (located/green). (ConceptLocation p.11, p.18)

questions.stem: To decide a module's local versus composite functionality, what activity does the deck say the analyst performs?

options.text: By executing the program under a profiler

questions.explanation: Local and composite functionality are determined by reading code and documentation. (ConceptLocation p.9)

questions.stem: Which statement about the four search marks is FALSE?

options.text: 'Next' means the class is scheduled for inspection
'Propagating' means inspected and the composite contains the concept
'Blank' means the class was inspected but the concept was not found
'Unchanged' means inspected and the composite lacks the concept

questions.explanation: Blank means NEVER inspected and not scheduled — not 'inspected but not found'. The other three statements are correct. (ConceptLocation p.11)

questions.stem: What is JHotDraw primarily designed to draw?

options.text: Technical and structured graphics such as network layouts and Gantt diagrams

questions.explanation: JHotDraw is a Java framework for drawing technical and structured graphics like network layouts and Gantt diagrams. (JHotDraw p.4)

questions.stem: JHotDraw traces its lineage to an earlier system. Which is correct?

options.text: It descends from HotDraw, written in Smalltalk by Kent Beck and Ward Cunningham
It descends from a C++ CAD tool by the Gang of Four
It was first written in Java by James Gosling
It descends from a Lisp editor by John McCarthy

questions.explanation: JHotDraw comes from HotDraw, originally developed in Smalltalk by Kent Beck and Ward Cunningham. (JHotDraw p.4)

questions.stem: Among early Java/Smalltalk projects, what specific 'first' does the deck credit JHotDraw with?

options.text: It was the first program ever written in Java
It was one of the first projects explicitly designed for reuse and labelled a framework
It was the first open-source project on GitHub

questions.explanation: JHotDraw was one of the first software projects explicitly designed for reuse and labelled a framework. (JHotDraw p.4)

questions.stem: Reverse-identify: a JHotDraw class extends AWT Frame, owns the current Tool and a Drawing, and is paired 1—1 with a view. Which class is it?
questions.explanation: DrawApplication (extends AWT Frame) owns the current Tool and a Drawing and is paired 1—1 with StandardDrawingView. (JHotDraw p.5)

questions.stem: Reverse-identify: this JHotDraw class extends AWT Panel, holds the selection, and receives notification from the Drawing. Which is it?
questions.explanation: StandardDrawingView (extends AWT Panel) is the view; it holds the selection and receives notification from the Drawing. (JHotDraw p.5)

questions.stem: In JHotDraw, one interface acts as the container that aggregates many Figures. Which interface fills that container role?
questions.explanation: Drawing is the figure-container interface that aggregates Figures 1..n (black-diamond composition). (JHotDraw p.5)

questions.stem: Reverse-identify: a JHotDraw interface for a drawable element that owns Handles 1..n and can itself contain other figures. Which is it?
questions.explanation: Figure is a drawable element with Handles 1..n that can itself contain figures (enabling Composite). (JHotDraw p.5)

questions.stem: In JHotDraw, the application keeps exactly one 'current' instance of which controller-role interface?
questions.explanation: Tool is the controller interface; the application has one current Tool. (JHotDraw p.5)

questions.stem: When you drag a small grip at the corner of a selected figure to resize it, you are using which JHotDraw element?

options.text: The menu items of the application
Interaction points (grips) on a figure for resizing/moving

questions.explanation: Handles are interaction grips on a figure (resize/move); a Figure has Handles 1..n. (JHotDraw p.5)

questions.stem: Which cardinality is stated for DrawApplication and StandardDrawingView?
questions.explanation: DrawApplication is associated 1—1 with StandardDrawingView. (JHotDraw p.5)

questions.stem: Which cardinality is stated between a Figure and its Handles?
questions.explanation: A Figure has Handles 1..n (1—1..n). (JHotDraw p.5)

questions.stem: You want your app to add a custom tool and a custom figure to JHotDraw. At the framework's extension points, how do you do this?

options.text: By editing the framework's source classes directly
By subclassing framework interfaces — e.g., MyTool/YourTool extend Tool, MyFigure/YourFigure extend Figure

questions.explanation: Applications subclass the framework interfaces (MyTool/YourTool extend Tool; MyFigure/YourFigure extend Figure). (JHotDraw p.7)

questions.stem: Applied to JHotDraw, what does the distinction between 'frozen spots' and 'hot spots' describe?

options.text: Frozen spots are bugs; hot spots are features
Frozen spots are tests; hot spots are production code
Frozen spots are the GUI; hot spots are the database
The framework's skeleton is fixed (frozen), while you plug in your own tools and figures (hot spots)

questions.explanation: Frozen spots = the fixed framework skeleton; hot spots = the application-specific tools/figures you plug in. (JHotDraw p.7)

questions.stem: If you point at JHotDraw's MVC 'Model', which classes are you pointing at?

options.text: Figures and their attributes plus Drawing (the figure container)

questions.explanation: Model = Figures and their attributes (FillColor, Position) plus Drawing. (JHotDraw p.17)

questions.stem: Reverse-identify the MVC role: in JHotDraw, DrawingView and DrawingWindow play which role?
questions.explanation: View = DrawingView (a clipping view of a window) and DrawingWindow. (JHotDraw p.17)

questions.stem: In JHotDraw's MVC, which elements act as the Controller?
questions.explanation: Controller = Tools that manipulate the model. (JHotDraw p.17)

questions.stem: A developer places a Tool inside the Model layer of JHotDraw's MVC. Why is that a mistake?

options.text: Tools are the View; the Model is Handles
Tools belong to no layer in MVC
It is not a mistake; Tools are part of the Model
Tools are the Controller; the Model is Figures/Drawing

questions.explanation: Tools are the Controller in JHotDraw's MVC; the Model is Figures/Drawing. Putting Tools in the Model is the documented mistake. (JHotDraw p.17)

questions.stem: A user clicks a figure in JHotDraw. Which ordering of MVC steps correctly traces what happens next?

options.text: View raises User Action -> Controller -> Update Model -> Model Notify -> View Update
Model raises User Action -> View -> Update Controller -> Controller Notify -> Model Update
Controller raises User Action -> Model -> Update View -> View Notify -> Controller Update
View raises Notify -> Model -> Update Controller -> Controller Action -> View Update

questions.explanation: The loop: View User Action -> Controller -> Update Model -> Model Notify -> View Update. (JHotDraw p.17)

questions.stem: A class implements the Tool (controller) interface and surfaces mouseDown/Drag/Up/Move in JHotDraw's MVC. Which class is it?
questions.explanation: AbstractTool implements the Tool (controller) interface and exposes mouseDown/Drag/Up/Move plus activate/deactivate. (JHotDraw p.18)

questions.stem: FigureAttributes in the MVC class picture keeps its attribute data inside which data structure?
questions.explanation: FigureAttributes holds attributes in an fMap:Hashtable. (JHotDraw p.18)

questions.stem: Of all possible sample codebases, what property of JHotDraw makes it especially suited to teaching concept location and pre/postfactoring?

options.text: It has no tests, so students can add them
It is tiny, with only a handful of classes
It deliberately avoids all design patterns
It is designed for reuse and pattern-dense, so concept location and pre/postfactoring can be taught cleanly

questions.explanation: A well-factored, pattern-based framework is exactly where change activities can be taught cleanly. (JHotDraw p.4, p.16)

questions.stem: You call move() on a grouped figure and every child shape moves. Which JHotDraw class delegates the call to all contained shapes?
questions.explanation: GraphicalCompositeFigure delegates methods to all contained Shapes, embodying Composite. (JHotDraw p.23)

questions.stem: JHotDraw's Factory Method use lets subclasses override two creation hooks on DrawApplication. Which two methods are they?
questions.explanation: DrawApplication defines createMenus() and createTools(); subclasses override them to add tools/menus. (JHotDraw p.43)

questions.stem: You want to give a figure both a border and a drop shadow. Which two concrete DecoratorFigure subclasses does JHotDraw supply?
questions.explanation: DecoratorFigure provides BorderDecorator and ShadowDecorator to add borders and shadows. (JHotDraw p.39)

questions.stem: Reverse-identify the pattern: in JHotDraw each tool is initialized with a prototypical figure and creates new figures by calling prototype.clone(). Which pattern is this?
questions.explanation: Each tool holds a prototype figure and clones it to create new figures — the Prototype pattern. (JHotDraw p.46-47)

questions.stem: Reverse-identify the pattern: a JHotDraw SelectTool holds ZeroClickState/OneClickState/TwoClickState so a tool behaves differently per click count. Which pattern is this?
questions.explanation: Externalizing a tool's mode via ZeroClickState/OneClickState/TwoClickState is the State pattern. (JHotDraw p.32)

questions.stem: Reverse-identify the pattern: JHotDraw separates layout algorithms (no-layout, align-top, align-left) from the figures being laid out, mirroring AWT/Swing layout managers. Which pattern is this?
questions.explanation: Pluggable, interchangeable layout algorithms separated from the figures is the Strategy pattern (the layouters). (JHotDraw p.28)

questions.stem: Reverse-identify the pattern: JHotDraw's LineConnection fixes the invariant 'connect figures with a line' algorithm and leaves variant steps as overridable empty methods (e.g. Petri-net vs. class-diagram connections). Which pattern?
questions.explanation: Defining the algorithm skeleton in LineConnection and deferring variant steps to subclasses is Template Method. (JHotDraw p.35)

questions.stem: Reverse-identify the pattern: in JHotDraw a composite figure and its child figures are treated uniformly so scale/rotate/move apply to all. Which pattern?
questions.explanation: Treating a composite figure and its children uniformly (figures composed of figures) is the Composite pattern. (JHotDraw p.22)

questions.stem: Reverse-identify the pattern: JHotDraw adds borders and shadows to a figure dynamically at runtime without subclassing each figure type. Which pattern?
questions.explanation: Attaching extra responsibilities (border/shadow) to a figure dynamically is the Decorator pattern. (JHotDraw p.39)

questions.stem: Scenario: you must add a brand-new figure type to JHotDraw with minimal impact. Which combination does the guide describe?

options.text: Subclass Figure / use the Composite hierarchy, register a Prototype to clone, and let Factory Methods wire it into menus/tools
Add a new State subclass to the SelectTool
Replace the MVC controller with a Strategy

questions.explanation: Adding a figure type: subclass Figure/Composite, register a prototype for cloning, and use factory methods to wire menus/tools. (JHotDraw p.23, p.43, p.47)

questions.stem: Scenario: you need a new tool MODE (e.g. an extra click behavior) with low impact. What does the guide recommend?

options.text: Add a Decorator to the figure
Add a State subclass rather than editing conditionals

questions.explanation: A new tool mode is added as a State subclass instead of editing large conditionals. (JHotDraw p.32)

questions.stem: Scenario: you want a new arrangement algorithm for composite figures without touching the figures. What does the guide recommend?
questions.explanation: A new layout is added as a Strategy (layouter) without touching figures. (JHotDraw p.28)

questions.stem: Scenario: you must support a new connection semantics, e.g. a Petri-net link. What does the guide recommend?

options.text: Override the variant hooks of the Template Method LineConnection
Add a Decorator to the connection

questions.explanation: A new connection semantics is supported by overriding the variant hooks of the Template Method LineConnection. (JHotDraw p.35)

questions.stem: Per the worked example, the watermark feature is most plausibly designed using which pattern(s)?

options.text: A Singleton holding the watermark text
A Decorator on figures, or a Template Method step in each format writer
An Iterator over the menu items
A Memento of the drawing state

questions.explanation: The guide suggests the watermark is likely a Decorator on figures or a Template Method step in each format writer. (JHotDraw p.39)

questions.stem: Which eight-pattern set does the deck single out as the ones JHotDraw leans on most heavily?

options.text: Singleton, Iterator, Visitor, Memento, Proxy, Flyweight, Bridge, Mediator
Adapter, Facade, Chain of Responsibility, Interpreter, Builder, Command
MVC, Composite, Strategy, State, Template Method, Decorator, Factory Method, Prototype

questions.explanation: The eight patterns the deck highlights in JHotDraw are MVC, Composite, Strategy, State, Template Method, Decorator, Factory Method, Prototype. (JHotDraw p.16)

questions.stem: When you add a feature to JHotDraw by subclassing at an extension point, why does the guide describe the resulting change as 'low-impact'?

options.text: Because JHotDraw has no dependencies between classes
Because the framework recompiles itself automatically
Because every change is a one-line edit
Each change plugs in a subclass at a designed extension point rather than editing the framework skeleton

questions.explanation: The patterns are change-enablers: each change adds a subclass at a hot spot, keeping impact small — the changeability the framework was designed for. (JHotDraw p.16)

questions.stem: Opening the JHotDraw demo application, which assortment of on-screen elements would you expect to see?

options.text: Tools, figures (ellipses/rectangles), connections, groups, text, annotations, images, and URL attachments
Only black-and-white line drawings with no text

questions.explanation: The demo GUI shows tools, figures, connections, groups, text, annotations, images, and URL attachments. (JHotDraw p.4, p.6)

questions.stem: Regarding which object owns the selection and who notifies whom, which statement about JHotDraw is correct?

options.text: The Drawing holds the selection and notifies the Tool
StandardDrawingView holds the selection and receives notification from the Drawing
The Tool holds the selection and notifies the Handle
DrawApplication holds the selection and notifies AWT

questions.explanation: StandardDrawingView (the view) holds the selection and receives notification from the Drawing. (JHotDraw p.5)

questions.stem: A student expects pages 49-50 of the JHotDraw deck to detail more patterns after the 'Other Design Patterns' divider. What do they actually find?

options.text: Detailed treatment of Observer and Visitor
A full UML model of the whole framework
No further pattern content; it is just a divider

questions.explanation: Pages 49-50 are an 'Other Design Patterns' divider with no further content. (JHotDraw p.49-50)

questions.stem: A new application 'ClassDiagrammDrawApplication' inherits from DrawApplication and overrides createTools() to add class/interface figure tools. Which pattern is being exploited?
questions.explanation: Overriding createTools()/createMenus() in a customized DrawApplication subclass is the Factory Method pattern. (JHotDraw p.43)

questions.stem: In JHotDraw's Prototype use, what does the Circle Tool do to create a new circle?

options.text: It clones a circle prototype figure
It decorates a generic figure with a circle border

questions.explanation: Each tool is initialized with a prototype figure; the Circle Tool clones a circle prototype. (JHotDraw p.46-47)

questions.stem: Which architectural claim about JHotDraw is FALSE?

options.text: Tools are part of the Model layer in JHotDraw's MVC

questions.explanation: Tools are the Controller, not the Model. The other three statements are correct. (JHotDraw p.5, p.17)

questions.stem: In the State example for a text field, what does the deck say 2 clicks do?
questions.explanation: In a text field: 1 click sets the cursor, 2 clicks select a word, 3 clicks select the whole line. (JHotDraw p.32)

questions.stem: The watermark request slide misspells the framework name. Which spelling appears?
questions.explanation: The watermark change-request slide mistypes JHotDraw as 'JHowDraw'. (ConceptLocation p.6)

questions.stem: Compare the JHotDraw roles: which mapping of class to MVC role is correct?

options.text: Figure/Drawing = Model; DrawingView = View; Tool = Controller
Figure = Controller; Tool = Model; DrawingView = View
Drawing = View; Tool = Model; Figure = Controller
DrawingView = Model; Figure = View; Tool = Controller

questions.explanation: Model = Figure/Drawing; View = DrawingView/Window; Controller = Tool. (JHotDraw p.17)

questions.stem: Which JHotDraw model class is the concrete implementation of the Drawing interface in the MVC class picture?
questions.explanation: In the MVC class picture, StandardDrawing implements the Drawing model interface (and AbstractFigure implements Figure). (JHotDraw p.18)

questions.stem: A maintainer wants to know the true underlying type of a figure wrapped by a BorderDecorator, but instanceof keeps returning the decorator type. What does the deck say about this?

options.text: instanceof always returns the wrapped figure's true type
Decorators are forbidden in JHotDraw for this reason
The decorated type is hard to obtain unless exposed via methods like getDecoratedType(); instanceof won't see through the wrapper
The wrapper deletes the original figure's type

questions.explanation: Decorator's documented caveat: the decorated type is hard to obtain unless exposed (e.g. getDecoratedType()); Java's instanceof won't see through the wrapper. (JHotDraw p.39-40)

questions.stem: Compare Composite and Decorator as used in JHotDraw: what is the key structural difference the deck draws?

options.text: Composite wraps one component; Decorator aggregates many children
Both wrap exactly one component identically
Composite aggregates many children to form a part-whole tree; Decorator wraps exactly one component to add responsibilities

questions.explanation: Composite holds many children (part-whole tree); Decorator wraps a single component and forwards to it while adding behavior. (JHotDraw p.20, p.38)

questions.stem: Why does the deck warn that you cannot rely on the compiler to stop a JHotDraw composite from holding the wrong kind of child?

options.text: You can't use the type system to restrict a composite's components, so you need run-time checks
Composites force all children to be the same class at compile time

questions.explanation: Composite's con: you can't use the type system to restrict which components a composite holds; enforce with run-time checks. (JHotDraw p.24)

questions.stem: JHotDraw's Composite allows figures structured how deeply?

options.text: Only a single flat list, no nesting

questions.explanation: Composite in JHotDraw allows nested structures of arbitrary depth. (JHotDraw p.23)

questions.stem: Which JHotDraw scenario best motivates the State pattern over a big conditional?

options.text: A figure composed of many child figures
A layout algorithm chosen at construction time
A zoom tool that toggles 'zoom in'/'zoom out' modes at runtime
Adding a border to a figure

questions.explanation: State externalizes a tool's mode (e.g. zoom in/out) so behavior changes at runtime without large conditionals. (JHotDraw p.32)

questions.stem: How many patterns are in the Gang-of-Four catalog, and into how many purpose categories are they split?
questions.explanation: The GoF catalog has 23 patterns in 3 categories by purpose (creational/structural/behavioral). (JHotDraw p.10, p.16)

questions.stem: Which is NOT one of the three underlying GoF design principles cited in the deck?

options.text: Program to an interface, not to an implementation
Find what varies and encapsulate it

questions.explanation: The three principles are: program to an interface; favor composition over inheritance; find what varies and encapsulate it. Premature optimization is not among them. (JHotDraw p.9)

questions.stem: Besides purpose (creational/structural/behavioral), GoF patterns are split along which second dimension?
questions.explanation: GoF patterns are split by purpose and by scope (Class vs. Object). (JHotDraw p.10-11)

questions.stem: Scanning the GoF purpose/scope table, exactly one creational pattern sits in the Class-scope column. Which one?
questions.explanation: Factory Method is the only Class-scoped creational pattern; Abstract Factory, Builder, Prototype, Singleton are Object-scoped. (JHotDraw p.12)

questions.stem: Builder, Prototype, and Abstract Factory share one GoF purpose category. Which?
questions.explanation: Builder, Prototype, Abstract Factory (and Factory Method, Singleton) are creational patterns. (JHotDraw p.12)

questions.stem: Bridge, Facade, and Proxy are grouped together under which GoF purpose category?
questions.explanation: Bridge, Facade, Proxy (and Adapter, Composite, Decorator, Flyweight) are structural patterns. (JHotDraw p.13)

questions.stem: Chain of Responsibility, Mediator, and Visitor share a single GoF purpose category. Which category groups them?
questions.explanation: Chain of Responsibility, Mediator, Visitor are behavioral patterns. (JHotDraw p.14)

questions.stem: In the GoF table, most behavioral patterns are Object-scoped; exactly two are Class-scoped. Which two?
questions.explanation: Interpreter and Template Method are the Class-scoped behavioral patterns; the rest are Object-scoped. (JHotDraw p.14)

questions.stem: Which GoF pattern is listed with BOTH Class and Object scope?
questions.explanation: Adapter is listed with both Class and Object scope (class/object variants). (JHotDraw p.11, p.13)

questions.stem: Which job does the deck assign to the 'structural' family of GoF patterns?
questions.explanation: Structural patterns decouple interface from implementation. Creational initialize/configure; behavioral handle dynamic interactions. (JHotDraw p.10)

questions.stem: Which job does the deck assign to the 'creational' family of GoF patterns?

options.text: Initializing and configuring classes and objects
Restricting which components a composite holds

questions.explanation: Creational patterns initialize/configure classes and objects. (JHotDraw p.10)

questions.stem: Which single slogan does the deck use to connect heavy design-pattern use to a framework's changeability?

options.text: Design patterns make code run faster
Frameworks should avoid patterns to stay simple
Extensibility of frameworks calls for extensive use of design patterns
Patterns are only for object-oriented databases

questions.explanation: The deck's thesis: extensibility of frameworks calls for extensive use of design patterns. (JHotDraw p.16)

questions.stem: A pattern lets clients treat an individual object and a tree-structured group of those objects through one uniform interface. Which GoF pattern is described?
questions.explanation: Treating individual objects and part-whole compositions uniformly is the Composite pattern's intent. (JHotDraw p.19)

questions.stem: Sketching the Composite structure, which four operations sit on the Component supertype?
questions.explanation: Composite's Component declares Operation, Add, Remove, GetChild; Composite forwards Operation() to each child. (JHotDraw p.20-21)

questions.stem: A pattern lets you swap one algorithm for another at the same call site without the client changing. Which GoF pattern is this?

options.text: Attach extra responsibilities to an object dynamically
Define a family of algorithms, encapsulate each, and make them interchangeable
Define an algorithm skeleton and defer steps to subclasses

questions.explanation: Strategy defines a family of algorithms, encapsulates each, and makes them interchangeable so the algorithm varies independently of clients. (JHotDraw p.25)

questions.stem: In a Strategy diagram, the Context object holds a reference to what?
questions.explanation: Strategy's Context holds a strategy:Strategy; ConcreteStrategyA/B/C implement AlgorithmInterface(). (JHotDraw p.27)

questions.stem: A pattern makes an object appear to change its class as its internal mode flips. Which GoF pattern is this?

options.text: Define a family of interchangeable algorithms
Create objects by cloning a prototype
Allow an object to alter its behavior when its internal state changes, appearing to change its class

questions.explanation: State lets an object alter its behavior when its internal state changes — the object appears to change its class. (JHotDraw p.30)

questions.stem: In a State diagram, when a client calls Request() on the Context, what does the Context do?
questions.explanation: State's Context holds state:State and delegates Request() to state->Handle(); ConcreteStateA/B implement Handle(). (JHotDraw p.31)

questions.stem: A pattern fixes an algorithm's overall skeleton in a base class while letting subclasses fill in particular steps. Which GoF pattern is this?

options.text: Wrap a component to add responsibilities dynamically
Define the skeleton of an algorithm, deferring some steps to subclasses

questions.explanation: Template Method defines the algorithm skeleton, deferring some steps to subclasses that redefine them without changing the structure. (JHotDraw p.34)

questions.stem: A pattern bolts extra responsibilities onto one object at runtime as a flexible alternative to subclassing. Which GoF pattern is this?

options.text: Define a family of interchangeable algorithms
Create objects by cloning a prototype
Attach additional responsibilities to an object dynamically — a flexible alternative to subclassing
Let subclasses decide which class to instantiate

questions.explanation: Decorator attaches additional responsibilities to an object dynamically, a flexible alternative to subclassing. (JHotDraw p.36)

questions.stem: In a Decorator diagram, how does the Decorator's Operation() ultimately produce its result?
questions.explanation: Decorator holds a component and forwards Operation() to component->Operation(); ConcreteDecoratorA/B add state/behavior. (JHotDraw p.38)

questions.stem: A pattern declares a creation method but lets each subclass decide which concrete class to instantiate. Which GoF pattern is this?

options.text: Define an interface for creating an object but let subclasses decide which class to instantiate
Clone a prototypical instance to create objects
Attach responsibilities to an object dynamically
Define a family of interchangeable algorithms

questions.explanation: Factory Method defines an interface for creating an object but lets subclasses decide which class to instantiate. (JHotDraw p.41)

questions.stem: In a Factory Method diagram, what does the ConcreteCreator's overridden factory method return?
questions.explanation: ConcreteCreator overrides FactoryMethod() to return new ConcreteProduct. (JHotDraw p.42)

questions.stem: A pattern produces new objects by copying a pre-built example instance rather than calling a constructor. Which GoF pattern is this?

options.text: Define an algorithm skeleton deferring steps to subclasses
Specify objects to create using a prototypical instance and create new objects by cloning it
Let an object change behavior with its state

questions.explanation: Prototype specifies the kinds of objects to create using a prototypical instance and creates new objects by copying/cloning the prototype. (JHotDraw p.45)

questions.stem: Per the deck's 'use when' list, which situation calls for Strategy?

options.text: When behavior must change as the object's internal state changes at runtime
When you need to clone objects specified at runtime
When you need part-whole tree structures
When many classes differ only in their behavior, or a class has many behaviors as conditionals

questions.explanation: Strategy: use when classes differ only in behavior, you need algorithm variants, an algorithm uses data clients shouldn't see, or a class has many behaviors as conditionals. (JHotDraw p.26)

questions.stem: Per the deck's 'use when' list, which situation calls for State?

options.text: When behavior depends on state and must change at runtime, or operations have large state-dependent conditionals
When many classes differ only in an algorithm
When you want to clone prototypes
When you need a flexible alternative to subclassing for visual embellishment

questions.explanation: State: use when behavior depends on state and must change at runtime, or operations have large multipart state-dependent conditionals. (JHotDraw p.30)

questions.stem: Per the deck's 'use when' list, which situation calls for Decorator?

options.text: When behavior depends on a runtime state machine
To add responsibilities to individual objects transparently and withdrawably, avoiding a subclass explosion
When classes to instantiate are specified at runtime via cloning
When you need a family of interchangeable algorithms

questions.explanation: Decorator: use when adding responsibilities to individual objects transparently, for responsibilities that can be withdrawn, or when subclassing would cause a subclass explosion. (JHotDraw p.37)

questions.stem: Per the deck's 'use when' list, which situation calls for Prototype?

options.text: When operations have large state-dependent conditionals
When you need to treat objects and compositions uniformly
When classes to instantiate are specified at runtime, to avoid a parallel factory hierarchy, or when instances have few state combinations
When many classes differ only in an algorithm

questions.explanation: Prototype: use when classes to instantiate are specified at runtime, to avoid a parallel factory hierarchy, or when instances have few state combinations. (JHotDraw p.45-46)

questions.stem: Per the deck's 'use when' list, which situation calls for Factory Method?

options.text: When you need to add responsibilities to an object dynamically
When behavior must change with internal state
When you need a family of interchangeable algorithms
When a class can't anticipate the class of objects it must create, or wants subclasses to specify created objects

questions.explanation: Factory Method: use when a class can't anticipate the class of objects it creates, wants subclasses to specify them, or to localize which helper subclass is the delegate. (JHotDraw p.41)

questions.stem: A tool must switch between 'zoom in' and 'zoom out' behavior at runtime as the user toggles a mode. Why is State the better fit than Strategy here?

options.text: State models behavior that changes with the object's internal state at runtime; Strategy is for selecting among algorithms chosen by the client
State requires no extra classes; Strategy requires many
They are interchangeable, so either is equally correct

questions.explanation: State and Strategy share a structure, but State is about behavior changing with internal state at runtime, while Strategy is about client-selected interchangeable algorithms. (JHotDraw p.25, p.30)

questions.stem: Given that Strategy and State look almost identical as class diagrams, what conceptual line does the deck draw to tell them apart?

options.text: Strategy is structural; State is creational
Strategy varies the algorithm independently of clients; State varies behavior as the object's internal state changes
Strategy uses cloning; State uses factory methods
There is no conceptual difference; only the names differ

questions.explanation: Both delegate to a swappable object, but Strategy is about interchangeable algorithms while State is about behavior tied to internal state changes. (JHotDraw p.25, p.30)

questions.stem: Which drawback does the deck list as a downside of choosing Strategy?

options.text: It forbids families of related algorithms
Clients must know the strategies, and there is Strategy-Context communication overhead
It makes algorithms impossible to swap

questions.explanation: Strategy cons: clients must know the strategies; Strategy-Context communication overhead; more objects. (JHotDraw p.29)

questions.stem: Which benefit does the deck list as an upside of Composite?

options.text: It uses the type system to restrict a composite's children
It prevents nesting beyond two levels
It removes the need for run-time checks
Client code accepting a primitive also accepts a composite, simplifying clients

questions.explanation: Composite pros: clients accepting a primitive also accept a composite; simpler clients; easy to add new component kinds. (JHotDraw p.24)

questions.stem: Which drawback does the deck list as a downside of Decorator?

options.text: A decorator and its component aren't identical, and you get lots of little objects

questions.explanation: Decorator cons: a decorator and its component aren't identical; lots of little objects. (JHotDraw p.40)

questions.stem: Which benefit does the deck list as a documented 'pro' of the State pattern?

options.text: It removes the need for any Context object
It localizes state-specific behavior and makes transitions explicit; new states are added as subclasses
It guarantees fewer total objects than a conditional
It prevents sharing of state objects

questions.explanation: State pros: localizes/partitions state-specific behavior; new states/transitions via new subclasses; transitions made explicit; state objects can be shared. (JHotDraw p.33)

questions.stem: Which consequence does the deck attribute to using Prototype in JHotDraw?

options.text: It forces a parallel factory hierarchy
It prevents application-specific classes from being used unmodified
It hides concrete product classes from clients and lets you add/remove products at runtime
It requires recompiling for each new product

questions.explanation: Prototype consequences: hides concrete product classes; lets clients use app-specific classes unmodified; add/remove products at runtime; specify new objects by varying values. (JHotDraw p.48)

questions.stem: Which consequence does the deck attribute to using Factory Method in JHotDraw?

options.text: It eliminates the need for any Creator class
It forbids subclasses from overriding creation
It clones prototypes instead of constructing objects
It provides hooks for subclasses and connects parallel class hierarchies

questions.explanation: Factory Method consequences: provides hooks for subclasses; connects parallel class hierarchies. (JHotDraw p.44)

questions.stem: Which assignment of three patterns to categories is fully correct?
questions.explanation: Singleton is creational, Adapter is structural, Observer is behavioral. (JHotDraw p.12-14)

questions.stem: Which list contains ONLY structural patterns?
questions.explanation: Adapter, Composite, Decorator, Facade (plus Bridge, Flyweight, Proxy) are structural. (JHotDraw p.13)

questions.stem: Which list contains ONLY behavioral patterns?
questions.explanation: Command, Iterator, Observer, Visitor (plus Interpreter, Template Method, Chain of Responsibility, Mediator, Memento, State, Strategy) are behavioral. (JHotDraw p.14)

questions.stem: Taken together, what single overarching goal do the deck's three GoF design principles serve?
questions.explanation: The principles aim at flexibility and reuse through decoupling. (JHotDraw p.9)

questions.stem: Once concept location hands over its classes, what two things does the Impact Analysis phase work out?

options.text: The change strategy and the impact set of classes affected
The natural-language wording of the user story
The export format writers to clone
The commit message for the new baseline

questions.explanation: Impact Analysis determines the change strategy and the impact set. (SoftwareChange p.9)

questions.stem: Starting from the concept-location classes, by what mechanism does Impact Analysis enlarge the impact set?

options.text: All classes in the project are added unconditionally
Class dependencies are analyzed and impacted classes are added to the impact set
The set is frozen and never grows

questions.explanation: Impact Analysis analyzes class dependencies and adds impacted classes to the impact set. (SoftwareChange p.9)

questions.stem: In the deck's design-phase figure, how is the impact set visualized?

options.text: A flat alphabetical list of all classes
A pie chart of maintenance categories
A 'Concept' at the centre with the impact set radiating outward through the software
A swim-lane diagram of Computer vs. Programmer

questions.explanation: The impact set is visualized as a Concept at the centre with the impact set radiating outward. (SoftwareChange p.7)

questions.stem: Which best captures the relationship between concept location and impact analysis?

options.text: Impact analysis finds the snippet; concept location grows it
Both produce the full impact set independently
Concept location finds the starting snippet; impact analysis grows it into the full impact set

questions.explanation: Concept location finds the starting class(es); impact analysis grows that initial set into the full impact set. (SoftwareChange p.8-9)

questions.stem: One of the four impacts on functionality is defined as reshaping structure while leaving observable behaviour untouched. Which impact is that?
questions.explanation: Refactoring changes software structure without changing behavior. (SoftwareChange p.4)

questions.stem: Why are BOTH prefactoring and postfactoring considered refactoring under the strict definition?

options.text: Each changes structure without changing behavior — before the change to minimize impact, after to remove anti-patterns
Each replaces one feature with another

questions.explanation: Refactoring = changing structure without behavior; prefactoring does this before (minimize impact), postfactoring after (remove anti-patterns). (SoftwareChange p.4, p.10, p.12)

questions.stem: A maintainer renames variables and extracts methods so the code reads better, with identical observable behavior and no new features. By the impact-on-functionality taxonomy, this is which type?
questions.explanation: Structural improvement with unchanged behavior and no new feature is refactoring. (SoftwareChange p.4)

questions.stem: Which definition of prefactoring matches the deck?

options.text: Refactoring done after the change to remove anti-patterns
Opportunistic refactoring that localizes (minimizes) the impact of the change on the software

questions.explanation: Prefactoring is opportunistic refactoring that localizes/minimizes the impact of the change. (SoftwareChange p.10)

questions.stem: What is the point of doing prefactoring ahead of writing any new feature code?

options.text: To make the upcoming change small and local
To verify the change after the fact
To remove anti-patterns the change introduced
To commit the change to version control

questions.explanation: Prefactoring happens before to make the upcoming change small and local. (SoftwareChange p.10)

questions.stem: Which trio of activities belongs to the Actualization phase?

options.text: Extracts concepts from the change request
Creates the new code, plugs it into the old code, and visits neighboring classes to update them
Commits the code and builds the baseline

questions.explanation: Actualization creates new code, plugs it into old code, and updates neighboring classes. (SoftwareChange p.11)

questions.stem: A change to one class forces edits in classes that depend on it, which in turn force more edits further out. Which Actualization phenomenon is this?

options.text: Change propagation as a deliberate act

questions.explanation: The ripple effect is changes cascading outward through dependencies. (SoftwareChange p.11)

questions.stem: Which GoF principle tells you to depend on abstractions rather than concrete classes?

options.text: Find what varies and encapsulate it
Program to an interface, not to an implementation

questions.explanation: 'Program to an interface, not to an implementation' is the principle about depending on abstractions. (JHotDraw p.9)

questions.stem: Which GoF principle motivates using object composition instead of deep inheritance hierarchies?

options.text: Program to an interface, not to an implementation
Find what varies and encapsulate it

questions.explanation: 'Favor composition over class inheritance' motivates composition over deep inheritance. (JHotDraw p.9)

questions.stem: JHotDraw isolates layout algorithms and tool modes behind interfaces so they can vary independently. Which GoF principle does this most directly express?

options.text: Find what varies and encapsulate it

questions.explanation: Isolating the varying parts (layouts, modes) behind interfaces expresses 'find what varies and encapsulate it'. (JHotDraw p.9)

questions.stem: What does Verification guarantee, and over which phases does it run?

options.text: It guarantees performance and runs only during Initiation
It guarantees security and runs only during Conclusion
It guarantees correctness of the change and runs across the implementation phases
It guarantees nothing and is optional

questions.explanation: Verification guarantees correctness of the change and runs continuously across the implementation phases. (SoftwareChange p.13)

questions.stem: Besides testing, what other activity does the deck list under Verification?
questions.explanation: Verification comprises testing (functional/unit/structural) AND walkthroughs. (SoftwareChange p.13)

questions.stem: Which trio of testing types does the deck explicitly list under Verification?
questions.explanation: The deck lists functional, unit, and structural testing under Verification. (SoftwareChange p.13)

questions.stem: In the Conclusion phase, what is committed and built?

options.text: The user story card is committed and a backlog is built
The finished code is committed to version control and a new baseline is built
The impact set is committed and a CDG is built
The test report is committed and a prototype is built

questions.explanation: Conclusion commits the finished code to version control and builds the new baseline. (SoftwareChange p.14)

questions.stem: After forking JHotDraw on GitHub for the ChangeReqLab, which collaboration workflow do students adopt?
questions.explanation: The ChangeReqLab has students fork JHotDraw and adopt GitHub flow. (ChangeReqLab p.1)

questions.stem: In the ChangeReqLab, where does the student place the user-story card in the workflow?

options.text: In a commit message on the main branch
In the README of the fork
In a CI pipeline configuration file
In the TODO backlog via GitHub Projects

questions.explanation: Students put a card in the TODO backlog via GitHub Projects as a portfolio artifact. (ChangeReqLab p.1)

questions.stem: The Conclusion phase explicitly ties the change process to which later course themes?
questions.explanation: Conclusion (commit, new baseline) ties into Version Control / Git and CI. (SoftwareChange p.14)

questions.stem: Which Lientz & Swanson category most directly maps to reducing technical debt by improving structure before problems arise?
questions.explanation: Preventive maintenance improves code structure to forestall future problems, closely related to refactoring and reducing technical debt. (SoftwareChange p.3)

questions.stem: Postfactoring removes a 'bloated class' a change introduced. In technical-debt terms, what is postfactoring doing?

options.text: Deferring all cleanup to the next release
Paying down debt the change just incurred by removing the introduced anti-pattern
Committing the bloated class as the new baseline

questions.explanation: Postfactoring eliminates anti-patterns (e.g. bloated class) the change introduced — paying down the freshly incurred debt. (SoftwareChange p.12)

questions.stem: Order these end-to-end activities: (i) write 'As a trial user, I want...', (ii) extract Export/Drawing/Text/Format, (iii) determine the impact set, (iv) commit and build the new baseline.
questions.explanation: Initiation/user story (i) -> Concept Location/extract concepts (ii) -> Impact Analysis/impact set (iii) -> Conclusion/commit-baseline (iv). (SoftwareChange p.5-14)

questions.stem: In JHotDraw's Strategy example, to what can a 'layouter' be attached?

options.text: A composite figure, to render it differently
A single Handle, to resize it

questions.explanation: Every layouter can be attached to a composite figure to render it differently (no layout, align-top, align-left). (JHotDraw p.28)

questions.stem: For JHotDraw's LineConnection, which part stays fixed across subclasses and which part each subclass changes?

options.text: Invariant: the connection semantics; varies: whether a line is drawn at all
